effects.text: On the first morning, {self.name} stood the line without flinching. The instructors noted it. So did the cohort.
{self.name} said nothing on the first morning, and watched everyone. By noon, {self.name} knew who would break.
{self.name} answered {rival.name} of {rival.house} before the whole cohort. The room laughed. {rival.name} did not.
{self.name} thanked {rival.name} for the counsel, smiled, and let the silence do the cutting. A feud was made — a quiet one.
{self.name} offered {rival.name} a hand instead of an answer. A rival who outranks you is also a ladder. {rival.name} took it, wary.
{self.name} let it pass. The cohort marked it. Some wounds you carry because the bill, paid now, costs more.
Steel under the academy lights. {self.name} left {rival.name} of {rival.house} on the floor, breathing, ruined in the only way that matters here: in front of everyone.
{self.name} disarmed {rival.name} and stepped back, blade lowered, before the cohort. Mercy, witnessed, is its own kind of victory — and {rival.name} knows the debt.
No blade. A word in the right ear, a forged lapse in {rival.name}'s record. {rival.name} fell, and never learned whose hand it was. {self.name} watched, untouched.
{self.name} went to {rival.name} of {rival.house} with no witnesses and no weapons, and asked what the quarrel had ever bought either of them. The feud died in a quiet room.
{comrade.name} slept through the watch. {self.name} stood it twice and said nothing to the instructors. {comrade.name} woke owing a debt neither of them named.
{self.name} reported the lapse. The watch is the watch; a line that bends once bends always. {comrade.name} took the punishment, and the lesson, and the grudge.
{self.name} kicked the cot until {comrade.name} stirred, swore, and stood. No record, no debt, no drama. Just two cadets and a long night.
Two cadets, a dead hour, and words that cannot be unsaid. {self.name} and {comrade.name} swore the old oath — blade, blood, and name. Sworn is not blood. Sometimes it is fiercer.
{self.name} swore it and meant every word to the marrow. {comrade.name} heard the difference. An oath like that is a gift and a chain at once.
{self.name} clasped {comrade.name}'s arm and named no oath. A friend, yes. But chains, even chosen ones, are still chains — and {self.name} keeps the hands free.

When the trial came, {self.name} sent {comrade.name} into the worst of it — the debt, called. {comrade.name} survived, and came back changed toward {self.name}. So did {self.name}. The crucible names what you are: Ruthless.
{self.name} took {comrade.name}'s place in the trial and the wounds that came with it. The debt was never called; it was forgiven. The crucible names what you are: Honorable.
{self.name} went in beside {comrade.name}, shoulder to shoulder, and let the debt dissolve into the smoke. They came out together. The crucible names what you are: Loyal.
{mentor.name} of {mentor.house} began to single {self.name} out — harder problems, colder critiques. Favor here is just higher expectation wearing a kinder face. {self.name} rose to it.
{self.name} made the climb deliberate — the right answers, the right deference, the right silences. {mentor.name} took {self.name} as a protege. Patronage is never free; the price comes later.
{self.name} declined the great {mentor.name}'s shadow. To rise on no one's favor is slower, and lonelier, and entirely your own. The cohort respected it. {mentor.name} did not forget it.
An hour of bad food and worse jokes in the mess. {self.name} laughed at someone else's expense and felt, briefly, like one of them.
{self.name} ate alone over a tactics primer. The cohort noticed the empty seat. So did {self.name}.

A junior cadet, {junior.name}, asked to serve under {self.name}'s banner — said he watched {self.name} stand the first formation and wanted nothing else since. A small loyalty. A small obligation. The way every house begins.
{self.name} set {junior.name} a hard task before saying yes. Loyalty offered cheap is worth what it cost. {junior.name} passed, and meant it more for the testing.
{self.name} sent {junior.name} back to find his own feet. Not cruelty — a kindness shaped like a refusal. A banner you are carried to is not yours.

The final trial, the whole cohort watching. {self.name} beat {best.name} of {best.house} fairly, and {best.name} — outdone — saluted. The best enemies are the ones who know your worth. {self.name} graduated first.

{self.name} took the trial by methods the rules did not cover. {self.name} graduated first; {best.name} graduated knowing exactly what {self.name} is, and waiting. Some victories are loans against the future.

{self.name} saw {best.name} was the better this day, and said so, before the cohort. To name a rival's excellence aloud costs pride and buys something rarer. {best.name} graduated first — and came to {self.name} after, an arm offered.

{self.name} traced the ruined kit and the missing orders back to {rival.name} of {rival.house}, and laid the proof before the proctors. {rival.name} kept rank but lost face — and learned that {self.name} fights with patience as well as pride.

{self.name} did not complain. {self.name} arranged that {rival.name}'s own next inspection failed worse, and untraceably. The cohort drew its own conclusions. A feud is a conversation; {self.name} answered in the same tongue.

{self.name} repaired the damage in silence, passed the inspection anyway, and named no one. The proctors saw a cadet who did not whine. {rival.name} saw something more frightening: a rival who could not be made to stumble.
{self.name} said nothing and did nothing, and let the wound sit. {rival.name}, emboldened, will go further. Some quarrels you starve; this one, {self.name} chose to feed.

{rival.name} of {rival.house} came back smaller than before, the shame still on them. {self.name} offered, quietly and with no audience, a way to stand up straight again. A rival you humble can be ruined — or rebuilt into the most grateful ally you own. {self.name} chose to rebuild.

{self.name} let {rival.name} reach for dignity — and took it again, in front of the same cohort. Twice broken, {rival.name} stopped being a rival and became something quieter and more dangerous: a person with nothing left to lose and one name to blame.
{self.name} walked past {rival.name} as though the feud, and {rival.name}, had already been forgotten. Sometimes indifference is the cruelest answer of all — and the one hardest to forgive.

{self.name} came to the infirmary where {rival.name} of {rival.house} lay mending, and brought no triumph — only an arm, and an end to it. The man you beat fairly and then forgave is the man who will hold the line beside you when it matters. Steel made the wound; this healed something older.
{self.name} sent a formal note of respect to the infirmary — correct, cold, complete. {rival.name} healed, and the quarrel cooled to mere dislike. Not friendship. But not a knife in the dark either.

{self.name} let the silence stand. {rival.name} mended alone, nursing the defeat into something colder. Victory taken and never softened is victory that keeps an enemy. {self.name} judged the enemy worth keeping.

{self.name} paid {proctor.name} of {proctor.house} what the rank was quietly worth, and the marks came back exactly as needed. A rung climbed in the dark is still a rung — and the man who sold it remembers the price, and that you were willing to pay it.

{self.name} thanked {proctor.name} for the consideration and declined to buy what should be earned. The proctor's face cooled. A door closed — but {self.name} owns every rung climbed after, free and clear.

{self.name} took the corruption to those above {proctor.name}, and watched a powerful man fall. The academy is cleaner for an hour. But a ruined proctor has friends, and a long memory, and now a name to teach them: {self.name}.

{self.name} wrote home plainly — the rivals, the cold nights, the slow climb. To set the truth down is to be, for a moment, a child writing to a house that existed before you and will outlast you. The reply, when it comes, will weigh more than any drill.

{self.name} wrote home a version where the victories shone brighter and the doubts did not appear. The house will be proud of a son who does not quite exist — and {self.name} will spend the rest of the term becoming him, or failing to.
{self.name} did not write. There are things the page cannot hold, and a distance that letters only sharpen. The house waits. {self.name} drills, and carries the name alone for a while longer.

{enemy.name} of {enemy.house} had been waiting for {self.name} a long time, and waited a little too openly. The matter ended the way these things end at the edge of the academy, where the lights do not reach. {self.name} walked back alone. A made enemy, unmade — and a weight that does not lift.

{self.name} did the harder thing: walked unarmed to the one person in the academy with the most reason to want {self.name} dead, and refused to make it simple. {enemy.name}, braced for a blade, got a hand instead. Not all wars end in winning. Some end in unmaking the reason to fight.

{self.name} did neither — only watched, and waited, and slept lighter. {enemy.name}'s hatred is a fact of the world now, carried out the academy gates into whatever comes next. Some debts you do not settle. You only live alongside them, eyes open.
{self.name} ran the drill past the point the instructors called it, into the part where skill is actually made. The body pays for it. The next battle will not.
{self.name} did the drill well and no more, and saved something for the long war that the academy only pretends to be a rehearsal for. Endurance is a skill too, and quieter.

{self.name}'s first command, and {self.name} took the worst of it personally — first into the danger, last out. {officer.name} and the others marked it forever: this is an officer who spends themselves before they spend us. The rank was earned the only way rank is ever truly earned, in the eyes of the people who must obey it.

{self.name} spent the command like coin and bought the objective with it. The mission succeeded, brilliantly, at a cost {officer.name} counted in faces. They will follow {self.name} anywhere now — out of respect for the results and fear of being the next line in the ledger. It is a kind of loyalty. The colder kind.

{self.name} won the action and then, in the report that went up the chain, named {officer.name} and the others before {self.name}'s own part in it. Glory shared is glory multiplied — every name {self.name} lifted is a person who will lift {self.name} in turn, for the rest of a career that has only just begun.

The post went to {self.name} on the record alone — cleaner service, harder results, an argument no board could refuse. {competitor.name} of {competitor.house} was passed over fairly, which is the most galling way to lose. They congratulated {self.name} with a face that promised this was not finished.

{self.name} did not win the post so much as ensure {competitor.name} lost it — a doubt planted, a past failure resurfaced at the perfect hour. The promotion is {self.name}'s. So is an enemy who knows exactly what happened and can prove nothing, which is the most dangerous kind to make.

{self.name} judged {competitor.name} the better fit for this post, this once, and said so to the board. The rank went elsewhere. But {competitor.name} of {competitor.house} learned that {self.name} could be trusted with the truth even when it cost — and there is no promotion in the service worth what that trust will return.

{superior.name} of {superior.house} gave {self.name} an order that was really a question — about loyalty, about how far {self.name} would go on another's word. {self.name} carried it out. The trust earned is real, and so is the thing {self.name} now knows about themselves, which does not go back in its box.

{self.name} refused — quietly, respectfully, immovably. {superior.name} lost a useful subordinate and gained a measure of a person who would not be bent, which powerful people both resent and, in their colder calculations, value. The promotion did not come. Something harder to name did.

{self.name} obeyed {superior.name} to the letter — and kept a copy of everything. The order was carried out; the promotion granted; and somewhere in {self.name}'s keeping now sits the proof of what {superior.name} asked, a knife that need never be drawn to do its work. Loyalty, with an exit.

The senior officer fell, the line wavered, and {self.name} did not wait for permission to do what permission would have been too slow to grant. By the time the chain of command caught up, {self.name} had already won the thing and worn the rank in all but paper. They made it paper. They had to.

{self.name} did not seize the empty command; {self.name} held the assigned post flawlessly and let the proper authority sort the rest. No glory, no overreach, no enemies made by stepping over anyone. Some careers are built in leaps. Others, quieter and harder to derail, are built exactly like this.

{admiral.name} of {admiral.house}, who has commanded fleets since before {self.name} could walk, chose {self.name} as the one to carry the doctrine forward. To be named heir to a great commander's thinking is rarer than blood-inheritance and harder to live up to. {self.name} rose to it, and the old admiral, watching, allowed themselves to be proud.

{self.name} did not wait to inherit {admiral.name}'s shadow; {self.name} stepped out of it, in public, with a victory the old admiral could not have won and would not soon forgive. The fleet has a new name to follow. The old one watches it rise the way the setting sun watches the dawn — and begins, quietly, to plan.

{self.name} took the governor's seat and governed as though the people on the ledger had faces, because they did. The province prospered slower and steadier than a harder hand would have driven it — and stayed loyal through the lean year that broke {self.name}'s harsher neighbors. Justice is a slow economy. It pays in crises.

{self.name} took the seat and ran the province like a well-tuned engine — every inefficiency trimmed, every yield maximized, the numbers immaculate. The treasury swelled. The people became, in {self.name}'s ledgers, a number that produces and occasionally complains. Both facts are true at once, and a governor must choose which to forget.

{self.name} treated the governorship as the opportunity everyone privately knows it to be, and the province paid {self.name} as much as it paid the throne. A fortune made. A reckoning postponed. Every governor who has done this has believed they would be the one to leave before the bill arrived. Most were wrong.

{self.name} won the policy fight against {rival_minister.name} of {rival_minister.house} on the merits — better figures, longer sight, the dull unanswerable force of being correct. The ministry is {self.name}'s. {rival_minister.name} retreated to nurse the particular grudge of those defeated by arithmetic they could not refute.

{self.name}'s policy was not the soundest, but it was the one the people could understand and cheer, and {self.name} sold it with a tongue {rival_minister.name} could not match. The ministry came on a wave of acclaim. Acclaim is a current; it carries {self.name} up now and will set the terms of the fall, if a fall ever comes.

Raised to the high treasury, {self.name} did the thing every predecessor had found reasons not to: cleaned it. Powerful people lost comfortable arrangements. {self.name} made the kind of enemies one only makes by being good at the job — and left the realm's finances, for the first time in a generation, telling the truth.

{self.name} took the treasury and understood it for what it truly is: not a vault but a web, every disbursement a thread to someone who now depends on {self.name}'s goodwill. {self.name} did not steal. {self.name} did something more lasting — became the person without whom nothing in the realm gets paid.

{self.name}'s first case, and {self.name} ruled as the law demanded rather than as power preferred — even when {defendant.name} of {defendant.house} had friends who expected otherwise. A reputation for incorruptible judgment is the slowest to build and the only one a magistrate cannot survive without. {self.name} began to build it here.

{self.name} found, in the law's gray margins, the verdict that served {self.name} best — {defendant.name} walked, gratefully, owing a debt that judges learn to collect. Justice is what {self.name} says it is, this once. Each time it gets easier, and each time the robe means a little less, and {self.name} tells themselves they will notice when to stop.

{self.name} made of {defendant.name} an example sharper than the crime required — a verdict meant to be talked about, and feared. The court learned what kind of magistrate it now served. So did everyone with a case yet to bring. Fear clears a docket fast. It also empties the room of justice, one frightened plaintiff at a time.

{self.name} prosecuted {accused.name} of {accused.house} — a power above {self.name}'s station, who had assumed station was armor — and made the conviction stick before the whole court. The realm learned that {self.name}'s law reaches upward. {accused.name} learned it too, and went to their punishment composing the long reply that the powerful always, eventually, compose.

{accused.name} of {accused.house} paid, and {self.name} found the law surprisingly accommodating. The acquittal advanced {self.name}'s career as a reliable friend to the powerful — which is a career, and a cage. {self.name} is now a person {accused.name} can ruin with a word, and both of them know it, and that knowledge is the actual price.

{self.name} took the praetor's robe on the strength of a record no one could gainsay, and {patron.name} of {patron.house}, who had expected to be thanked for the elevation, found {self.name} owed them nothing but courtesy. A clean ascent. The first power-holder in {self.name}'s life who cannot call in a debt for it — which is its own kind of wealth.

{patron.name} of {patron.house} placed the robe on {self.name}'s shoulders, and the weight of it was partly cloth and partly obligation. {self.name} rose faster for the patronage and will answer for it later, as one always answers — at the patron's convenience, on a matter of the patron's choosing, for a price set the day the gift was given.

{self.name} sought out {rival.name} of {rival.house}, whom {self.name} had beaten to the post, and offered them a place worth having under {self.name}'s own command. The defeated rival, expecting cold triumph, got an open hand instead. The ones you best and then elevate become the ones who guard your back — because they have seen you choose generosity when cruelty was free.

{self.name} kept {rival.name} close the other way — watched, reported on, every move shadowed by people {rival.name} would never spot. {self.name} sleeps easier knowing where the grudge is. {rival.name}, who might have let it fade, will eventually feel the surveillance, and resent it, and become exactly the threat {self.name} was watching for. Suspicion has a way of building its own enemies.

{self.name} let {rival.name} fade into the background of a busy career — neither befriended nor watched, simply outpaced. Most grudges die of neglect. A few do not. {self.name} has chosen, without quite deciding to, to find out which kind this one is, later, when it is no longer convenient.

Offered a leap into a different track — a soldier courted for the senate, a steward for the fleet — {self.name} declined and went deeper into the mastery already begun. The realm is full of capable people spread thin across three ambitions. {self.name} chose to be undeniable at one. Depth is its own kind of power, and a rarer one.

{self.name} took the leap into the unfamiliar track, trading hard-won seniority for a wider board to play on. The old colleagues called it folly; the old skills count for little in the new arena. But the figure who commands more than one domain frightens those who command only one — and fear, wielded well, is the shortest road to the top of any of them.

{mentor.name} of {mentor.house} grew old and outranked, the way every mentor eventually does, and {self.name} made sure the world remembered what {self.name} owed them — a place of honor, a public word, the respect a lesser climber would have economized on. The student who lifts the teacher up at the end is the student who proves the teaching took.

{self.name} took the post that had been {mentor.name}'s, in the end — not unkindly, but unmistakably, the pupil seated where the teacher once sat. {mentor.name} smiled and meant most of it. There is a grief in being surpassed by what you made, and a pride in it, and {mentor.name} felt both, and {self.name} pretended not to see the first.

No glory in it, no event worth the telling — only the unending paperwork, the inspections, the small correct decisions that no one notices until one of them is wrong. {self.name} did the work. Careers are not made of set-pieces. They are made of the days between them, done right.

{self.name} pushed the routine down to those who should be learning it, freeing {self.name}'s own hours for the decisions only {self.name} could make. The art of rising is partly the art of knowing what to stop doing. {self.name} is learning to lead instead of merely to work.
{self.name} raised the tithe a third again. The coffers breathe. In the field-towns, a harder winter is doing its sums, and writing {self.name}'s name in the ledger of grievances that houses inherit.

{self.name} sold off a holding to balance the books — solvent today at the cost of the house being smaller tomorrow. The tenants there learned, by letter, that they now belong to someone else. A house that sells its land to pay its debts is a house going quietly backward.

{lender.name} of {lender.house} was happy — too happy — to help. The credits arrived within the hour. So did the smile of a man who has just acquired something more useful than coin: a hold over {self.name}, to be called in at the worst possible moment, which is the only moment such men ever choose.

{self.name} cut the household's own spending before the people's — sold the plate, dismissed the musicians, ate as the garrison ate. It raises little, and costs much in pride. But a lord who tightens their own belt first is remembered for it, in the only ledger that ever protects a house: the people's.

{self.name} paid {creditor.name} of {creditor.house} every credit owed, on the day demanded, with interest counted to the hour. It emptied the treasury and bought something a treasury cannot: the end of the obligation, and the quiet respect of a hard man who expected a harder time collecting.

{self.name} could not pay in coin, so {creditor.name} of {creditor.house} named the other price: a vote, a posting, a door opened. The debt is cleared and a new kind of obligation takes its place — the kind that does not appear in any ledger, and never quite ends.

{self.name} made the debt disappear — the records, the witnesses, the leverage. {creditor.name} of {creditor.house} arrived to collect and found nothing to collect on, and a rival who had simply decided not to owe. The ledger is clean. The enemy is not.

{self.name} poured materials into the forge-world's failing reactor before it failed. Expensive, unglamorous, invisible if it works — which is the whole nature of stewardship. The foundry-hands, who know exactly how close it came, will not forget who chose them over the budget.

{self.name} ran the reactor past its margins and banked the output it should have spent on repairs. It held — this time. Every overrun the steward survives teaches the dangerous lesson that the next one will hold too. The foundry-hands work a little faster now, and a little more afraid.

{self.name} sold the dying reactor to a buyer who did not ask questions, and let the forge-world become someone else's problem before it became a disaster. A clean profit, taken at exactly the right moment. The thousands who worked it woke to a new flag and an old fear.

{self.name} caught {steward.name} of {steward.house} skimming the accounts and made it public, and painful, and final. The thieving stops; the recovered coin returns; and every other steward in the house now serves an honest master out of fear, which is a colder loyalty than love but a more reliable one.

{self.name} let {steward.name} return the coin and resign with their name intact, sparing a desperate man the ruin a harder lord would have made of him. Mercy bought quietly is rarely seen and never advertised. But {steward.name} will tell one person the truth of it, and that person will tell another.

{self.name} did not stop the skimming. {self.name} simply made it a partnership, with terms. {steward.name} keeps a smaller share and a much larger fear, and the house's accounts now run two sets of books. Profitable. Until the day someone above {self.name} learns to read them both.

{self.name} heard the delegation out and granted the relief — remitted tithes, grain from the house stores, the small mercies that cost a lord coin and buy a people's patience. It is expensive. It is also the difference between subjects who grumble and subjects who rise.

{self.name} answered the unrest with the garrison. Order returned to the streets by nightfall, swift and total and never forgiven. Fear is the cheapest tool a lord owns, and the one that compounds the steepest interest. The quiet now is the quiet before, not after.

{self.name} gave the crowd a name to hate that was not {self.name}'s — a local official, a foreign merchant, anyone whose ruin would cost the house nothing. The anger found its target and spent itself there. A magician's trick: the hand is faster than the mob, this once.

The agri-worlds came in rich this season. {self.name} banked the surplus against the lean years that always, eventually, come. Stewardship is mostly the dull art of remembering that good seasons are loans the bad ones call in.

{self.name} let the field-towns keep more of a fat year than custom required. A small thing, soon forgotten by the ledger — and long remembered by the people, who keep their own accounts, in a currency that does not spend but does protect.

A distant market convulsed and the shock rolled outward, as they do, taking a bite of the house's holdings before it passed. {self.name} weathered it the way houses weather everything: by being older than the panic, and patient enough to outlast it.

While others sold in fear, {self.name} bought — holdings, stockpiles, contracts, all of it cheap in the panic and dear again by spring. The crash that ruins the timid enriches the cold. {self.name} ended the season larger than it began.

{self.name} bought {auditor.name} of {auditor.house} the way the rot is always bought — quietly, expensively, and only ever for a while. The books came back clean. The price of a clean book is one more person who knows it isn't, and now owns you a little.

{self.name} opened the true books to {auditor.name} before the auditor could find them, named the rot, and named the cure. It costs standing to confess. It costs more to be caught. A house that cleans itself, publicly and painfully, is a house that survives the cleaning.

{self.name} handed {auditor.name} a culprit — a steward, a clerk, someone whose ruin would satisfy the ledger without troubling the family name. The audit closed. Somewhere a lesser house took the fall for a greater one's habits, which is the oldest arithmetic there is.

{self.name} called for volunteers, and a world that remembers being treated fairly answered with its sons. Goodwill is the only resource that converts directly into the willingness to die for you. {self.name} spent some of it, knowing it does not refill on command.

{self.name} asked, and a grateful world gave — freely, which is the only kind of giving that doesn't breed resentment. They will give again, if {self.name} remembers not to ask too often. The art of being loved by those you tax is the whole art of lasting rule.

{self.name} took nothing, this season, from the world that had given so much. The steward thought it madness; the people thought it something rarer. A lord who can take and chooses not to is a lord people will follow past the point where fear would have failed.

A surveyor's report, a strange seam in a worthless rock, a thing the markets call exotic because they cannot agree what else to call it. {self.name} sold the find to a collector who paid in the kind of coin that does not ask where things come from. Profit, clean and immediate. Some doors, once you take the money, you can no longer choose not to have opened.

{self.name} ordered the find catalogued, crated, and kept — not sold, not studied, just held, the way wise houses hold things they do not yet understand. There is a patience that looks like greed and a greed that looks like patience. Only time tells which this was.

{self.name} kept the find and set the house's best minds to it, against the steward's protest that curiosity has never once balanced a ledger. Perhaps not. But the houses that endure are not always the richest — sometimes they are merely the ones who looked closely at the thing everyone else sold.

{self.name} filled the thinned ranks the fast way: the press-gangs went out, and the field-towns gave up sons they had not offered. The garrison is whole again. Each man in it is a family that did not choose this, and remembers, and waits.

{self.name} paid for swords instead of pressing them. Mercenaries fight well and ask no questions and stay exactly as long as the coin does — which is the entire problem with them, discovered always at the worst possible moment, never the bill's.

{self.name} did not find more men. {self.name} found a way to need fewer — redrawn patrols, abandoned strongpoints not worth their cost, a thinner line held smarter. It is the steward's true art, this: making scarcity look like strategy, until the day it stops being a choice.

{self.name} traded standing for coin through {broker.name} of {broker.house} — a contract steered, a rival's bid quietly spoiled, the kind of favor that leaves no fingerprints and fills the treasury. Influence is wealth that has not yet decided what to become. {self.name} decided for it.

{self.name} let the favors sit and compound, declining {broker.name}'s easy conversions. The poor spend influence as they earn it; the powerful let it accumulate until a single call can move a fleet or break a house. {self.name} is learning the patience of the latter.

{self.name} called in every debt at once, through {broker.name}, and the network paid out in a single overwhelming season — and then was empty, every favor spent, every well dry. A king's gambit. It works precisely once, and {self.name} had better have spent it on something worth never being able to do again.

{claimant.name} of {claimant.house} produced a debt {self.name}'s grandfather had sworn and never paid — old paper, faded ink, binding all the same. {self.name} honored it. A name is the one possession that crosses the grave, and a house that pays its dead's debts is a house whose word, living, is worth something.

{self.name} took the old debt to the magistrates and argued it into dust — technicalities, dead witnesses, the long memory of lawyers paid to forget. {claimant.name} left with nothing but a grievance, which in this world is never quite nothing. The debt is void. The enmity is now {self.name}'s own, freshly minted.

{self.name} did not argue. {self.name} simply refused — the dead made no promises {self.name} would honor, and {claimant.name} could do what they liked about it. It is cleaner than the courts and far more dangerous. A repudiated creditor does not appeal. They wait, and they plan, and they remember the face.

Raiders in the trade-lanes, and a price named for letting the convoys pass. {self.name} paid it, the way the practical have always paid it, and the grain got through and the people ate. Tribute is the tax you pay for the wars you cannot afford to fight. It is also a promise to the wolf that the sheep are reachable.

{self.name} refused tribute and funded the hunt instead — dearer, bloodier, and final in a way that paying never is. The lanes ran red, then clear. The merchants who use them now use {self.name}'s name like a charm. The wolf that is killed sends no second bill.

{self.name} did not pay the raiders, nor hunt them. {self.name} hired them — turned the wolf to the leash, pointed it at rivals' lanes instead of {self.name}'s own. It is brilliant and it is filthy and it works until the leash frays, which it always, eventually, does.

{self.name} kept the grain-dole flowing through a thin month — a small standing cost, a small standing mercy. The people do not cheer a dole. They simply do not riot, which is the quiet dividend a wise steward learns to value above applause.

{self.name} trimmed the dole to save a little. The ledger thanks {self.name}; the breadlines lengthen by a few, and a few is how every great unrest begins — never as a flood, always as a handful of people deciding they have had enough.

texts.title: The First Morning
texts.body: Dawn over the academy, and a thousand young nobles arranged into something the instructors will spend years breaking and remaking. The cohort stands in the cold, breath fogging, every house's pride packed into one shivering line. They are watching each other already. This is where the rivals and friends of a lifetime are made — and it has not yet been five minutes.

choices.stand_tall: Stand the line without a flicker. Let them see nothing to exploit.
choices.measure_them: Say nothing. Watch everyone. Learn who breaks first.

texts.title: Words in the Barracks
texts.body: The cohort falls quiet as {rival.name} of {rival.house} stops in your path. The words are perfectly courteous — a remark about your house, your rise, your right to stand among the old blood — and perfectly designed to wound, delivered with a smile the whole barracks can see. To answer is to begin something. To not answer is also to begin something. There is no version of this where nothing begins.

choices.humiliate: Cut {rival.name} down so cleanly the whole cohort remembers it.
choices.cold_courtesy: Thank {rival.name} warmly. Let the silence do the cutting.
choices.extend_hand: Offer a hand. A rival who outranks you is also a ladder.
choices.swallow_it: Let it pass. Some bills cost less paid later — or more.

texts.title: The Reckoning
texts.body: It has been coming since the barracks. {rival.name} of {rival.house} has nursed this quarrel like a coal, and now the cohort expects an ending — they always do. The thing about a feud in a place like this is that everyone is watching to see what you are made of, and the answer, whatever it is, will follow you out the gates and into every command you ever hold.

choices.duel: Steel, under the lights, in front of everyone. End it the old way.
choices.duel_just: Best {rival.name} — then lower the blade where all can see. Mercy, witnessed.
choices.ruin_quietly: No blade. A word in the right ear. Let {rival.name} fall and never know your hand.
choices.make_peace: Go to {rival.name} alone, unarmed, and ask what the quarrel ever bought.

texts.title: The Empty Post
texts.body: Third watch, the dead hours, and {comrade.name}'s post is empty — you find them dead asleep in the bunkroom, a punishment offense if the instructors catch it, and they will catch it. {comrade.name} is no one important yet. But no one is born important here; they are made it, by who stands where, on cold nights like this one.

choices.cover_for_them: Stand the watch twice. Say nothing. Let them wake owing you.
choices.report_them: Report the lapse. A line that bends once bends always.
choices.wake_them: Kick the cot until they stand. No record, no debt, no drama.

texts.title: The Old Words
texts.body: It is late and you and {comrade.name} are the last two awake, and the conversation has drifted to the place such conversations drift — to the question of who, in all of this, you can actually trust. {comrade.name} means it. You can hear that they mean it. Blood you are given; this you choose. The old oath is not spoken lightly in this academy, because here, more than anywhere, people remember what was sworn.

choices.swear: Swear the old oath. Blade, blood, and name.
choices.swear_deep: Swear it to the marrow, and mean every word past the point of safety.
choices.decline_gently: Clasp their arm. Name no oath. Keep your hands free.

texts.title: The Blooding
texts.body: The trial they do not warn you about: the one where the academy stops pretending to be a school. Live ordnance, real stakes, and a moment where the only way through puts someone in the fire — and {comrade.name}, who owes you for the watch you stood, is standing right where you'd send them. The crucible is about to name what you are. It always does. That is the point of it.

choices.call_the_debt: Send {comrade.name} into the worst of it. The debt, called. (Names you: Ruthless)
choices.shield_them: Take {comrade.name}'s place and the wounds with it. The debt, forgiven. (Names you: Honorable)
choices.share_the_fire: Go in beside {comrade.name}, shoulder to shoulder. (Names you: Loyal)

texts.title: The Instructor's Eye
texts.body: You have been noticed. {mentor.name} of {mentor.house} — who has forgotten more war than the cohort will ever learn — has begun to watch you the way a buyer watches a blade. In a place built to grind nobles into uniformity, being seen is the rarest currency there is. It is also a door that, once opened, is very hard to choose how far to walk through.

choices.earn_it: Meet the harder problems head-on. Earn the eye by deserving it.
choices.court_it: Make the climb deliberate. Court the patronage — and the price.
choices.keep_distance: Decline the great one's shadow. Rise on no one's favor but your own.

texts.title: The Mess
texts.body: An hour of bad food and worse company — the one stretch of the day the instructors don't own. The cohort sprawls and argues and brags. You could lose yourself in it, or use it.

choices.trade_stories: Trade stories. Laugh. Be, for an hour, one of them.
choices.study_alone: Take a corner and a primer. Sharpen while they rest.

texts.title: A Banner to Follow
texts.body: A junior cadet — {junior.name}, younger, nervous, earnest in the way that has not yet been beaten out of him — asks to serve under your banner. He says he watched you stand the first formation and has wanted nothing else since. It is a small thing. Every great house was once a small thing, and someone's first yes.

choices.accept: Accept. A small loyalty, a small obligation — the way houses begin.
choices.test_first: Set a hard task first. Loyalty offered cheap is worth what it cost.
choices.decline: Send him to find his own feet. A banner you're carried to is not yours.

texts.title: The Deciding Trial
texts.body: The last trial, and the whole cohort in the gallery to watch it. It comes down, as everyone knew it would, to you and {best.name} of {best.house} — the one rival the academy could never decide was beneath you. Whoever takes this graduates first, and first means the first command, the first eyes of the great houses, the first rung of everything after. {best.name} is very, very good. That is what makes it worth winning.

choices.win_clean: Beat {best.name} fairly, on the merits, in the open.
choices.win_dirty: Take it by methods the rules don't cover. First is first.
choices.yield_to_better: Name {best.name} the better, this day, aloud. Some things cost more than losing.

texts.title: The Spoiled Kit
texts.body: Your kit fails inspection — straps cut where they shouldn't fray, your orders gone from a locker only you and the proctors hold keys to. The instructors mark you down cold; they do not care who did it, only that it was done. But you know. Everyone in the cohort knows. {rival.name} of {rival.house} is careful enough to leave no proof and careless enough to let you see the satisfaction. A feud has graduated from words to deeds. So, now, must your answer.

choices.prove_it: Trace it, prove it, lay it before the proctors. Patience as a weapon.
choices.answer_in_kind: Don't complain. Arrange that {rival.name}'s next inspection fails worse — untraceably.
choices.endure_and_excel: Repair it in silence, pass anyway, name no one. Be unbreakable.
choices.let_it_fester: Say nothing, do nothing. Let the wound sit — and the quarrel grow.

texts.title: The One You Broke
texts.body: {rival.name} of {rival.house} carries the shame you put on them like a limp — the cohort sees it, and so, every morning, do you. They are diminished now, a rival reduced to a cautionary tale told about you. Which is precisely the dangerous moment. A person with nothing left to protect is a person who can do anything. What you do with someone you've already broken decides whether they become your most loyal ally or your most patient enemy.

choices.offer_a_door: Offer them, quietly, a way to stand up straight again. Rebuild what you broke.
choices.grind_them: Let them reach for dignity — then take it again. Break them for good.
choices.ignore_them: Walk past as though they were already forgotten. The cruelest answer.

texts.title: The Infirmary
texts.body: {rival.name} of {rival.house} is mending in the academy infirmary — your work, fairly done, before the whole cohort. The feud is over; you won it clean. What remains is the question the victory left behind, the one no instructor will grade: a beaten rival is a resource or a reservoir of hatred, and which one is decided not on the duelling floor but here, in a quiet room, by whether you come at all.

choices.visit_the_infirmary: Come in person. Bring no triumph — only an arm, and an end to it.
choices.send_word: Send a formal note of respect. Correct, cold, complete.
choices.say_nothing: Let the silence stand. A victory unsoftened keeps its enemy.

texts.title: A Quiet Arrangement
texts.body: {proctor.name} of {proctor.house} finds you after the evening drill — all warmth and confidence, an arm around the shoulder. The academy, the proctor explains in the tone of a man doing you a favor, has always understood that talent and patronage walk together; that a rank can be recognized early, for those wise enough to recognize the recognizer. The offer is never stated outright. It does not need to be. The proctor smiles, and waits, and the whole weight of how things really work leans on the silence.

choices.take_the_offer: Pay what the rank is quietly worth. A rung climbed in the dark is still a rung.
choices.refuse_clean: Decline to buy what should be earned. Close the door, keep your name.
choices.expose_it: Take it to those above the proctor. Watch a powerful man fall — and remember you.

texts.title: A Letter Home
texts.body: A rare quiet hour, and a blank page, and the weight of the name at the top of it. Somewhere out among the stars your house waits to hear how its blood fares in the crucible — the house that existed before you, that will go on after, that you carry whether you chose to or not. What you write, and what you leave out, is its own small act of who you intend to be.

choices.honest_account: Write it plainly — the rivals, the cold, the slow climb. The truth.
choices.burnished_account: Write the version where the victories shine and the doubts don't appear.
choices.no_letter: Write nothing. Some things the page can't hold. Carry the name alone a while.

texts.title: The Marked Man
texts.body: There is someone in this academy who wants you dead. Not the figurative wanting of rivals — the literal kind, earned by something you did or chose, and now {enemy.name} of {enemy.house} watches you the way you have learned to watch exits. It cannot go on. One of you will end it, one way or another, before the term does. The only choice left is which kind of person walks out the gates: the one who finished it, the one who unmade it, or the one who learned to sleep with one eye open.

choices.finish_it: End it where the lights don't reach. A made enemy, unmade — and a weight that stays.
choices.disarm_the_grudge: Walk to them unarmed. Refuse to make it simple. Unmake the reason to fight.
choices.outlast_it: Neither. Watch, wait, sleep lighter. Live alongside the hatred, eyes open.

texts.title: The Drill Yard
texts.body: The yard again, the same forms, the same aching repetition the academy believes turns nobles into officers. It mostly does. The only variable the instructors leave you is how much of yourself you spend on it.

choices.push_hard: Run it past where they call it. Make skill the hard way.
choices.pace_yourself: Do it well and no more. Save something for the long war.

texts.title: The First Command
texts.body: It comes, as it comes to all who rise: the first time other lives hang on your word. A handful of people — {officer.name} among them — now look to you for the orders that will send some of them home and some of them nowhere. The rank on your collar is paper. The command is the thing you must earn from the only authority that matters, the eyes of the people who must obey it, in the next few hours.

choices.lead_from_front: Take the worst of it yourself. First into danger, last out.
choices.command_coldly: Spend the command like coin to buy the objective. Results over faces.
choices.share_the_glory: Win it, then name them before yourself in the report.

texts.title: The Contested Post
texts.body: A post has opened above you, and you are not the only one reaching for it. {competitor.name} of {competitor.house} wants it as badly, deserves it nearly as much, and stands precisely where you mean to stand. Promotions like this are never just about the better candidate; they are about what each of you is willing to do to be chosen, and what that doing will cost you both afterward.

choices.win_on_merit: Win it on the record alone. The most galling way to beat someone.
choices.undermine_rival: Ensure they lose it. A doubt planted, a failure resurfaced.
choices.defer_gracefully: Tell the board they're the better fit, this once. Buy something rarer.

texts.title: The Test
texts.body: {superior.name} of {superior.house} gives you an order, and you understand at once that the order is not the point. It is a question wearing an order's clothes — about how far you will go on another's authority, about whether you are the kind of subordinate that powerful people can truly use. Your answer will define the rest of your service under them, and something quieter that you will have to live with regardless.

choices.obey: Carry it out. Earn the trust; learn the thing about yourself.
choices.refuse_on_principle: Refuse, respectfully, immovably. Lose the post, become unbendable.
choices.obey_then_leak: Obey to the letter — and keep a copy of everything. Loyalty with an exit.

texts.title: The Empty Command
texts.body: The senior officer is down, the situation will not wait for the chain of command to deliberate, and the gap where authority should be is standing open in front of you. No one has given you this. No one is going to, in time. The rank is there for the taking — by whoever has the nerve to take it and the skill to be proven right.

choices.seize_the_moment: Take the command without waiting for permission. Win it, make it paper.
choices.hold_the_line: Hold your assigned post flawlessly. No overreach, no enemies made.

texts.title: The Admiral's Shadow
texts.body: {admiral.name} of {admiral.house} has commanded fleets since before you were born — a living institution, the kind of figure whose doctrine outlives them. And lately the old admiral has been watching you the way the great watch for an heir. To stand in that shadow is shelter and stricture both. To step out of it is a thing the ambitious eventually must do, and the grateful never quite forgive themselves for.

choices.earn_their_trust: Become the heir to their thinking. Rarer than blood, harder to deserve.
choices.eclipse_them: Step out of the shadow with a victory they couldn't have won.

texts.title: The Governor's Seat
texts.body: A province is offered into your keeping — a real one, with real people, real harvests, real grievances that will now have your name on them. How you govern it is the first true statement of what kind of power you intend to wield, made not in a battle or a courtroom but in the daily, unwatched arithmetic of whose interests come first.

choices.govern_justly: Govern as though the ledger had faces. Slow, steady, loyal in crises.
choices.govern_efficiently: Run it like an engine. Immaculate numbers; the people become one of them.
choices.govern_for_profit: Take the opportunity everyone knows it to be. A fortune; a postponed reckoning.

texts.title: The Policy Fight
texts.body: A ministry hangs on a single contested policy, and across the chamber {rival_minister.name} of {rival_minister.house} argues the opposite case with everything they have. The seat will go to whoever's vision prevails. You have the figures to win it on truth — or the tongue to win it on something warmer and less durable.

choices.sound_policy: Win on the merits. The dull, unanswerable force of being correct.
choices.popular_policy: Win on the policy people can cheer. Ride the wave; mind the fall.

texts.title: The High Treasury
texts.body: You have risen to where the realm's true money lives, and found what everyone who reaches this room finds: that the treasury is less a vault than a quiet conspiracy of comfortable arrangements, each one defended by someone powerful. You can clean it, and make enemies of the worst sort — the kind you earn by doing the job well. Or you can learn what it is really for.

choices.reform_the_treasury: Clean it. Make the enemies that competence earns.
choices.consolidate_power: Become the person without whom nothing in the realm gets paid.

texts.title: The First Case
texts.body: The robe is new on your shoulders and the first case is already harder than they warned: {defendant.name} of {defendant.house} stands before you, and the law points one way while power, friendship, and your own future all murmur another. Every magistrate's reputation is built or broken in the early rulings, when the realm is still deciding what kind of judge you are.

choices.rule_by_law: Rule as the law demands, whatever power prefers. Build the slow reputation.
choices.rule_by_favor: Find the verdict that serves you. Collect the debt judges learn to collect.
choices.rule_harshly: Make an example sharper than the crime. Clear the docket with fear.

texts.title: The Great Trial
texts.body: The case of your career so far: {accused.name} of {accused.house}, a power above your station, who walked in assuming that station was armor — and may be right, unless you prove otherwise before the whole court. Convict them and the realm learns your law reaches upward. Let them buy their way clear and you learn what your robe is really worth.

choices.convict_the_mighty: Make the conviction stick. Show the realm your law reaches up.
choices.accept_the_bribe: Let them pay. Advance as a reliable friend to the powerful — and their hostage.

texts.title: The Praetor's Robe
texts.body: The praetorship is within reach, and {patron.name} of {patron.house} stands ready to place the robe on your shoulders — for they have backed your rise, and a backed rise is rarely a free one. You can take it clean, on a record that needs no patron, or take it faster on the strength of a debt you will be paying for years.

choices.take_robe_clean: Take it on your record alone. Owe this one no debt.
choices.take_robe_owed: Rise faster on the patronage. Answer for it later, at their convenience.

texts.title: The One You Beat
texts.body: {rival.name} of {rival.house} reached for the same post you took, and lost — and now serves on, a competent person nursing the specific resentment of someone passed over. They are not your enemy yet. Whether they become one, or become something better, depends largely on what you do now that you've won.

choices.offer_a_post: Offer them a place worth having under you. Win the back-guard, not just the post.
choices.watch_them: Keep them under quiet surveillance. Sleep easier; perhaps build the threat you fear.
choices.ignore_them: Let them fade into a busy career. Most grudges die of neglect. Most.

texts.title: The Crossroads
texts.body: You have risen far enough to be courted by a different world entirely — the soldier wooed toward the senate, the steward toward the fleet, the advocate toward command. The leap means trading hard-won seniority for a wider board and a steeper climb. To stay is to deepen a mastery already real. To go is to gamble it on becoming something rarer: a person who commands more than one domain.

choices.stay_the_course: Go deeper. Be undeniable at one thing. Depth is its own power.
choices.change_tracks: Take the leap to a new arena. The figure who commands two domains frightens those who command one.

texts.title: The Teacher, Outgrown
texts.body: The one who taught you has grown old, and you have grown past them — the natural, melancholy arithmetic of any real mentorship. {mentor.name} of {mentor.house} is outranked now, by you among others, and the moment has come to decide what you do with a debt that can no longer be repaid in kind, only in honor or in its absence.

choices.honor_them: Make the world remember what you owe them. Prove the teaching took.
choices.surpass_them: Take the post that was theirs. Unkindly is not the word. Unmistakably is.

texts.title: The Days Between
texts.body: No crisis today, no rival, no test — only the work. The inspections, the reports, the hundred small correct decisions that earn no story and prevent a dozen. This is what a career actually is, between the moments anyone remembers.

choices.do_the_work: Do the work. Set-pieces are rare; the days between are the career.
choices.delegate_it: Push the routine down. Learn what to stop doing. Lead, don't just labor.

texts.title: The Lean Season
texts.body: Your steward lays the accounts before you with the particular flat calm of a man delivering bad news he has rehearsed. The treasury runs dry within a few turns at the present rate. The numbers are not in dispute; only what you do about them is. Every choice on the table balances the books and unbalances something else — the people, the house's future, your own freedom — because in stewardship there is no such thing as free money, only debts wearing different faces.

choices.raise_the_tithe: Raise the tithe. The coffers breathe; the field-towns remember.
choices.sell_a_holding: Sell a holding. Solvent today, smaller tomorrow.
choices.take_the_loan: Take {lender.name}'s loan. Coin now — and a hold over you, later.
choices.austerity: Cut your own household first. Raises little; costs pride; the people see it.

texts.title: The Debt Called
texts.body: It comes, as you knew it would, at the worst possible moment — that being the only moment a man like {creditor.name} of {creditor.house} ever chooses. The loan that saved you is called in, with the warm and terrible courtesy of someone who holds all the cards and wants you to admire how gently they play them. The debt is real. The question is what currency you settle it in, and what you are willing to become to be free of it.

choices.pay_in_full: Pay it in coin, in full. Clean, costly, done.
choices.pay_with_favor: Pay in favor — a vote, a posting, a door. Trade one debt for a deeper one.
choices.refuse_to_pay: Make the debt disappear. Records, witnesses, leverage — all of it. Keep the enemy.

texts.title: The Failing Reactor
texts.body: Word from the forge-world: the main reactor is past its life and praying its way through each shift. The foundry-master's report is blunt where such reports usually flatter — invest now, or gamble that it holds long enough to stop being your problem. The thing about infrastructure is that doing it right is invisible and doing it wrong is a catastrophe with names attached. Stewardship is mostly the choice to be unthanked.

choices.invest_now: Pour materials in before it fails. Expensive, invisible, right.
choices.risk_the_output: Run it past its margins and bank the output. It held last time.
choices.cut_it_loose: Sell the dying reactor to a buyer who won't ask. Profit now, disaster later — elsewhere.

texts.title: Two Sets of Books
texts.body: The discrepancy is small, then consistent, then unmistakable: {steward.name} of {steward.house}, who has kept your accounts faithfully for years, has been keeping a little of them too. The sums are not ruinous. The principle is. How a lord answers theft from within the house tells the whole house what kind of master they serve — and the answer, once given, is very hard to revise.

choices.make_example: Make it public and final. Honesty enforced by fear is still honesty.
choices.quiet_dismissal: Let them return the coin and resign with their name. Mercy, unadvertised.
choices.take_a_cut: Don't stop it — partner in it, on your terms. Profitable. Until it isn't.

texts.title: The Delegation
texts.body: They have come a long way to stand in your hall — a delegation from a world where the unrest is no longer a number in a report but a thing with faces, hungry and frightened and, beneath both, angry. They are respectful. They are also a warning wearing its best clothes. What you grant or refuse here travels home with them tonight, and becomes the story that world tells about your house for a generation.

choices.grant_relief: Grant relief — remitted tithes, grain from the stores. Expensive patience.
choices.send_the_garrison: Answer with the garrison. Order by nightfall, never forgiven.
choices.blame_a_scapegoat: Give the crowd a name to hate that isn't yours. The hand beats the mob, once.

texts.title: A Fat Year
texts.body: The agri-worlds came in heavy this season — more grain, more credits, more breathing room than the projections dared. A good problem, and a revealing one: what a steward does with a surplus says more than what they do with a shortfall.

choices.bank_it: Bank it against the lean years. Good seasons are loans the bad ones call in.
choices.share_it: Let the field-towns keep more than custom requires. Forgotten by the ledger, not the people.

texts.title: The Crash
texts.body: A market somewhere distant has convulsed, and the shock is rolling outward through every house with holdings in its path — yours among them. Panic is its own weather. Some are ruined by it; some, colder, are made.

choices.weather_it: Weather it. Be older than the panic, patient enough to outlast it.
choices.buy_the_dip: Buy while others sell in fear. The crash that ruins the timid enriches the cold.

texts.title: The Audit
texts.body: {auditor.name} of {auditor.house} arrives with the polite implacability of someone who already knows the answer and only wants to watch you say it. The house's books have grown a second life beneath the first — small diversions, useful lies, the ordinary rot of running anything for long enough. The auditor will find it. The only question left is what you do in the hours before they do.

choices.buy_silence: Buy the auditor. The books come back clean — and one more person owns you.
choices.confess_and_reform: Open the true books first. Name the rot, name the cure. Survive the cleaning.
choices.blame_the_steward: Hand them a culprit whose ruin won't touch the family name.

texts.title: The World That Remembers
texts.body: There is a world among your holdings that you have treated well, season after season — and worlds, like people, keep accounts. Its governor's letter is warm in a way official letters never are, and beneath the warmth is an offer: this world would give you something, gladly, because of who you have been to it. Goodwill is the rarest thing a steward banks, because it is the only resource that volunteers.

choices.call_the_levy: Call for volunteers. A fair-treated world answers with its sons.
choices.request_tribute: Ask for tribute. They give freely — if you don't ask too often.
choices.let_them_keep_it: Take nothing this season. Be the lord who can take, and doesn't.

texts.title: The Strange Seam
texts.body: A surveyor's report crosses your desk, flagged and uncertain: something has been found in a place that should have held nothing — a seam, a signature, a substance the assayers can price but not name. The markets would call it exotic and pay accordingly. Your steward sees coin. Something older in you sees a question. They are not the same thing, and the choice between them is the choice of what kind of house yours becomes.

choices.sell_to_collector: Sell it to a collector who won't ask where it came from. Clean profit.
choices.keep_it_quiet: Catalogue it, crate it, keep it. Hold what you don't yet understand.
choices.study_it: Set your best minds to it. The enduring houses looked closely at what others sold.

texts.title: The Thin Line
texts.body: The garrison commander's report is a study in restraint: the lines are held, technically, by men who have not been relieved in too long across too much ground. Manpower has run short, and shortage of soldiers is the kind that gets quiet right up until it gets catastrophic. You need bodies for the wall. Where they come from is its own decision, with its own bill.

choices.conscript: Send the press-gangs. The wall is whole; the field-towns remember.
choices.hire_mercenaries: Pay for swords. They stay exactly as long as the coin does.
choices.thin_the_line: Find a way to need fewer men. Make scarcity look like strategy.

texts.title: The Broker
texts.body: {broker.name} of {broker.house} deals in the one currency that doesn't mint: influence, favor, the soft power of who owes whom. They have come with the usual proposition — that your accumulated standing could be so much more useful as something else, if you'd only let it move. Influence is wealth in its most patient form. Spending it is easy. Knowing when is the whole game.

choices.spend_influence: Trade standing for coin. Favors that leave no fingerprints.
choices.hoard_influence: Let it sit and compound. The powerful accumulate; the poor spend.
choices.call_in_everything: Call in every debt at once. A king's gambit — it works exactly once.

texts.title: The Old Paper
texts.body: {claimant.name} of {claimant.house} comes bearing a debt older than you — paper your grandfather signed, an obligation sworn by a man now dust and never honored. The ink has faded. The claim has not. Nothing forces your hand here except the thing that has always bound noble houses to their own history: the name at the top of the page, which is also the name at the top of yours.

choices.honor_it: Pay your grandfather's debt. A house that pays its dead's word keeps its own.
choices.dispute_it: Argue it to dust in the courts. The debt voids; the enmity is freshly yours.
choices.repudiate_it: Simply refuse. The dead made no promise you'll keep. They'll wait, and plan.

texts.title: Wolves in the Lanes
texts.body: Raiders have found your trade-lanes, and with them the oldest proposition in commerce: a price for safe passage, named by men with the ships to enforce it. The convoys carry grain your people need. Paying is practical and quick and a quiet confession that your sheep are reachable. The alternatives cost more, in coin or blood, and settle more.

choices.pay_tribute: Pay for passage. The grain gets through; the wolf learns your address.
choices.fund_a_hunt: Refuse, and fund the hunt. Dearer, bloodier, final. The dead wolf sends no bill.
choices.hire_them: Hire them. Point the wolf at your rivals' lanes. Brilliant, filthy, temporary.

texts.title: The Dole
texts.body: A thin month, and the standing question every steward faces in thin months: the grain-dole that keeps the poorest fed costs a little, every cycle, forever. It buys no glory and earns no thanks. It only prevents things — which is the unglamorous heart of governing well.

choices.fund_the_dole: Keep it flowing. A small cost, a small mercy, no riot.
choices.trim_the_dole: Trim it to save a little. The breadlines lengthen by a few.

specializations.name: Mining Outpost
specializations.//: MATERIALS, lean & sterile. Cheap, safe (no pop=no unrest), low ceiling. The reliable backbone of a forge economy. Stack mines (building) on top.

specializations.name: Shipyard World
specializations.//: MATERIALS powerhouse but EXPENSIVE & populated. Net materials huge, but heavy credit upkeep means it bleeds you when insolvent (and then unrest hits its manpower). The military house's crown jewel and its biggest liability. Most slots of any spec.

specializations.name: Agri-Collective
specializations.//: MANPOWER home, fast-growing population. Food feeds people; people are soldiers and unrest both. Highest popGrowth = most manpower over time AND most exposure to unrest spirals. Cheap to run.

specializations.name: Fortress-Station
specializations.//: Pure UPKEEP SINK, zero yield — the §17 'marginal in most runs, decisive in some'. You build it for defense/projection (a future military-layer hook), not income. Sterile, so no unrest. A fortress economy is a bet that survival outvalues growth.

specializations.name: Research Enclave
specializations.//: EXOTICS home — the ONLY steady exotics source in the catalog, so decisive for any exotics-dependent path (and the M5 anomaly systems later). Costly, low raw output, sterile. Exotics are marginal until they aren't; this is the spec that makes them a strategy.

specializations.name: Trade Consortium
specializations.//: CREDITS home — highest raw credit yield in the catalog. Populated (carries unrest), and upkeep is paid in MANPOWER not credits, so it stays profitable even when broke but quietly drains your soldiers. The merchant house's engine.

specializations.name: Senatorial Seat
specializations.//: INFLUENCE home — the intrigue house's whole economy. Produces almost no material wealth; converts a credit cost into soft power. Useless to a forge house, decisive to a schemer who turns influence into everything else (see steward 'the_influence_broker'). Sterile.

buildings.name: Deep-Core Drill
buildings.//: Doubles down on a mining_outpost's materials. Cheap upkeep; pure materials scaling for a forge economy.

buildings.name: Orbital Foundry
buildings.//: Big materials+credits on a shipyard_world, but steep one-time cost and upkeep — amplifies both the yield and the insolvency risk that defines the spec. High-roller military infrastructure.

buildings.name: Conscription Hall
buildings.//: Turns an agri_collective's population into soldiers faster. Pushes the manpower path hard — but more conscription is more unrest pressure on a populated world. Power with a political cost.

buildings.name: Bastion Batteries
buildings.//: No yield — a defensive build-out for the fortress_station (hooks the future military layer). Materials sink that buys safety, not income. Deliberately 'useless' on a spreadsheet, decisive in a siege.

buildings.name: Exotics Laboratory
buildings.//: The only way to meaningfully scale exotics. Expensive (even costs exotics to build), but for an exotics strategy it's the multiplier that makes the resource real. Sterile, high-upkeep.

buildings.name: Free Port
buildings.//: Scales a trade_consortium's credits, upkeep again paid in manpower — keeps printing money while broke, keeps draining soldiers. Doubles down on the merchant tradeoff.

buildings.name: Patronage Office
buildings.//: UNIVERSAL (no requires) — any holding can buy soft power. The only non-senatorial influence source, so it lets a material house dip a toe into intrigue. Converts a credit stream into influence.

buildings.name: Public Granary
buildings.//: UNIVERSAL unrest-mitigation. No resource yield — its 'output' is social stability on a populated world (pairs with the steward unrest events; treat as a standing reason to keep a world calm). The build-out for a humane or cautious economy.

scenarios.//: SCENARIO 2 of 3 — THE CIRCLING HEIR (inheritance path). Fantasy: born to power, but the wealth makes you a target — relatives and vassals already maneuvering. Treasury rich (the OPPOSITE economic game from the fallen house: influence-heavy, the intrigue economy). VOCAB FLAGGED (§8 strawman): nature Ambitious/Proud/Cruel/Loyal; aptitudes Schemer/Orator/Paranoid/Diplomat; skills charisma/oratory/intrigue/tactics; ambitions 'seize_the_throne'/'master_the_senate'/'outshine_all_rivals'. era 'fractured_stars'. Confirm/swap at §8 lock.

houses.name: House Serval
houses.//: Influence-RICH, materials-thin: the intrigue house economy (pairs with 'the_influence_broker', senatorial_seat). A different game from Voss entirely — your power is people and favor, not forges.

characters.name: Lucia Serval
characters.//: Heir on the law/intrigue track — starts at rank 2 (privilege of birth), so the contested-promotion/great-trial events are immediately in range. Ambitious+Schemer = plays the long game.

characters.name: Castor Serval
characters.//: The elder cousin who believes HE should have been heir — same rank as Lucia, blood bond but hostile disposition. The knife in the family (brief §3 'a cousin who'd poison you'). Pre-armed for feud/succession tension.

characters.name: Vera Serval
characters.//: The loyal aunt-regent who actually runs things — outranks Lucia, can bind to mentor/superior roles. Loyal+Diplomat = the steadying hand, and the advisor whose framing the player trusts (maybe too much).

characters.name: Oren
characters.//: Lowborn-raised sworn man, no house name of his own (uses house tag for engine but is 'Oren' only) — the chosen-bond foil to Castor's blood-betrayal. The friend who'd die for you vs. the cousin who'd poison you, both in one family.

relationships.//: Blood bond, low disposition — family that maneuvers against you. The succession fault-line.
Castor resents harder than Lucia suspects — asymmetric, a betrayal waiting to bind.
The sworn bond — fiercer than the blood ones around her. The brief's 'sworn can be fiercer than blood' made literal.

holdings.name: Highmarch
holdings.//: A wealthy populated seat — the prize everyone's circling. trade_hub + market = the credit/influence flow that makes Serval worth scheming over.

holdings.name: The Spire
holdings.//: A second, prestige holding — research_station signals an old, established house with resources to spare on knowledge.

scenarios.//: SCENARIO 1 of 3 — THE FALLEN HOUSE (restoration path). Fantasy: one poor holding, debts, contempt from those who rose past you; climb back. Treasury deliberately credit-poor (hooks 'the_lean_season' immediately). VOCAB FLAGGED FOR REVIEW (from §8 strawman): nature traits Proud/Vengeful/Just; aptitudes Strategist/Administrator; skills tactics/discipline/administration/charisma; ambition 'restore_house_fortunes'. era 'fractured_stars'. Confirm or swap when §8 locks.

houses.name: House Voss
houses.//: Credit-poor on purpose: starts near insolvency to trigger steward crisis events early. Materials/manpower survive from better days.

houses.name: House Harrow
houses.//: The risen rival who took what Voss lost — wealthier, contemptuous. The antagonist house.

characters.name: Aldric Voss
characters.//: Proud+Vengeful = feels the family's fall as personal wound (drives the §13 contempt-for-the-risen). Rank 1 already (a half-step up) so career events are reachable.

characters.name: Mira Voss
characters.//: Younger sibling, the steward to Aldric's soldier. Just+Administrator = the conscience and the ledger. A blood bond worth protecting (and a vulnerability).

characters.name: Drusus Harrow
characters.//: The risen rival, outranks Aldric (rank 3 vs 1) — binds to 'a_rival_who_outranks_you' roles. Cruel/Ambitious, the face of the contempt.

relationships.//: The sibling bond — the protagonist's strength and soft spot.
Pre-loaded enmity — feeds rivalry/feud events from turn 1.
Drusus disdains rather than hates — he barely thinks of Voss as competition yet. Asymmetric, per the brief.

holdings.name: Greywater
holdings.//: One poor agri holding — barely feeds the house. Low yield, some unrest already. The single rung to climb from.

holdings.name: Anvil's Rest
holdings.//: Harrow's wealth engine — what a forge-world buys you, and what Voss lacks.

scenarios.//: SCENARIO 3 of 3 — THE RISEN OFFICER (merit path). Fantasy: no name, no land, only talent — the low-born who out-fights the aristocracy that despises them (the core Red Rising register from the brief §3). Treasury minimal (almost no house wealth — you ARE the asset, not your holdings). The title/office GAP (§4) is the whole drama: high skill, near-zero rank/birth. VOCAB FLAGGED (§8 strawman): nature Ambitious/Just/Loyal/Cruel; aptitudes Strategist/Tactician/Brilliant; skills tactics/discipline/leadership/gunnery; ambitions 'earn_a_command'/'become_legend'/'outshine_all_rivals'. era 'fractured_stars'.

houses.name: Vane
houses.//: Barely a house at all — almost no treasury, ZERO influence (the lowborn has no favor-network). The fantasy: you have nothing but yourself. Every resource event bites hard here.

houses.name: House Calderon
houses.//: The great house Kessa serves under and is despised by — the aristocracy that holds rank she'll have to take by sheer merit.

characters.name: Kessa Vane
characters.//: THE GAP (§4): careerRank 2 but skills maxed for her tier — a better officer than the dukes above her, and resented for it. Ambitious+Just+Brilliant = rises on merit and refuses to become what she's fighting. Highest skill-load of any protagonist; lowest birth.

characters.name: Tiberius Calderon
characters.//: THE GAP inverted: rank 5 (Admiral) by birth, but tactics 1 — 'a useless duke a low-born admiral could outfight' (§4 verbatim). Outranks Kessa massively; binds to superior/admiral roles. The contemptuous aristocrat she must serve and surpass.

characters.name: Juno Calderon
characters.//: The WORTHY rival — highborn but actually good (unlike her uncle the duke). Outranks Kessa by one. The brief's 'worthy enemy' / LotGH dignity: a rival you could respect. Disposition neutral-ish, could go either way (feud OR sworn). The richest relationship to leave open.

characters.name: Dorn
characters.//: The grizzled lowborn sergeant who rose with Kessa from nothing — sworn bond, the found-family the merit path builds instead of inheriting. Loyal+Quartermaster = keeps her supplied and honest.

relationships.//: She resents serving a fool who outranks her; he barely registers her. The §4 birth-contempt wound, the engine that drives the whole path.
He DOES notice her — as an upstart who needs putting in her place. Mutual, which makes it volatile.
Deliberately near-zero: the worthy rival could become friend or nemesis depending on play. The most open relationship in any scenario.
Juno secretly respects the talent — slightly positive, the seed of a possible sworn bond across the class line.
Strongest sworn bond of the three scenarios — the lowborn's chosen family is fiercer than any blood, because it's all she has.

holdings.name: Drydock Nine
holdings.//: Not a domain — a granted billet, a fortress posting with almost no population or economy. She holds a position, not land. The merit path starts with a JOB, not an inheritance.
